summary: Gruber argues that Apple is entirely justified in blocking Beeper Mini's unauthorized access to iMessage, framing iMessage not as a mere protocol but as a premium service Apple operates at significant cost exclusively for its device owners. He details how Beeper has effectively lost the cat-and-mouse game with Apple, now requiring users to jump through absurd hoops involving jailbroken iPhones or Mac-generated registration codes. Using an extended analogy comparing iMessage to American Express Centurion Lounges, he argues Beeper is essentially forging credentials to access a premium perk they have no right to. He then dismantles criticism from senators like Elizabeth Warren and a bipartisan congressional letter to the DOJ, arguing their framing ignores that cross-platform encrypted messaging already exists through WhatsApp and Signal. Gruber's core position is that being really good at competing is not anticompetitive behavior.
key_points: Beeper Mini has effectively lost the cat-and-mouse game with Apple, now requiring jailbroken iPhones or Mac-generated registration codes that will relegate it to obscurity
iMessage is a full service requiring servers, bandwidth, content moderation, and handling trillions of messages per year — not merely a protocol that can be reverse-engineered without consequence
The Centurion Lounge analogy frames iMessage as a premium perk for Apple device owners, making Beeper's unauthorized access equivalent to presenting forged credentials
Congressional criticism from Warren, Klobuchar, Lee, and others ignores that WhatsApp, Signal, and other free encrypted messaging apps are readily available on all platforms
Blocking an unauthorized client from accessing a proprietary service is not anticompetitive — the messaging app market is genuinely competitive with Apple hosting all competitors in its App Store
money_quotes: Half-working interop for a messaging service might as well be not working at all.
iMessage is much more than a protocol — it's a service.
Beeper Mini presenting itself as Messages on a Mac to gain access to iMessage is as dishonest as presenting a forged Amex Platinum Card to gain access to a Centurion Lounge.
Centurion Lounges aren't free and neither is iMessage.
Keeping an exclusive feature exclusive is not 'squashing competitors'.
There is only one iMessage — that's the protocol and platform Apple runs. Beeper Mini users aren't separate from 'iMessage users'; Beeper Mini users became iMessage users.
Being really good at competing is not anticompetitive.
The attention to copy editing in that sentence is indicative of the amount of thought put into the letter as a whole.
key_insight: iMessage is a premium service Apple builds and operates at enormous cost for its device owners, not a public utility — and blocking unauthorized clients from freeloading on that infrastructure is competition, not anticompetitive behavior.
